If this is `ImageData.RootFS.Layers`, that corresponds to layers’ `UncompressedDigest` values, not `ID`; `ID` is either randomly generated or built from a sequence of `RootFS` values (in `storageImageDestination.Commit`). (Note that the same layer (same `DiffID`=`UncompressedDigest`) may be a part of two entirely different layer sequences - ABC and DEC share one layer but are different filesystems).

My first guess would be to build all of this from `storage.Image.TopLayer` and the `storage.LayerParent` links – using the structure that containers/storage has already built out of the raw data; to be fair, I haven’t actually implemented it so there may be a snag.

@rhatdan @mtrmac  I think we need to come to conclusion first for "what we want from output." by interpretation of Image by layers from local repository.

e.g on remote registry there is image `app1:latest` with layers as follows.
```
alp-layer-0  --- alpine:latest  (root)
alp-layer-1  --- alpine:latest
app-layer-0  --- app1:latest
app-layer-1  --- app1:latest
new-layer-0  --- app2:latest
new-layer-1  --- app2:latest
```

#### scenario's :
- `` podman pull app1:latest`` is done and `alpine:latest` is not present in local registry.
- tree command will following output. 
   ``` 
     $ podman image tree app1:latest
     └─ ID: 03fcf20a2010  Tags: [app1:latest] VirtualSize: 2.2MB
    ```
   - _This is correct as all layers belong to `app1:latest`_
- now if we pull `podman pull alpine:latest` and run the  `tree` command
    ```
     $ podman image tree app1:latest
     └─ ID: 3e778775ad17  Tags: alpine:latest] VirtualSize: 2MB
        └─ ID: 03fcf20a2010  Tags: app1:latest] VirtualSize: 212B
    ```
     - _Since, we have information of `alpine` image also, so this output is also correct._
- pull image app2:latest   `podman pull app2:latest` and run tree command for `app2`
    ```
     $ podman image tree app2:latest
     └─ ID: 3e778775ad17  Tags: [alpine:latest] VirtualSize: 2MB
        └─ ID: 03fcf20a2010  Tags: [app1:latest] VirtualSize: 212B
           └─ ID: d9cff78213c7  Tags: [app2:latest] VirtualSize: 266B
    ```
- Now if delete `app1` and do a tree command on `app2`
    ```
     $ podman image tree app2:latest
     └─ ID: 3e778775ad17  Tags: [alpine:latest] VirtualSize: 2MB
        └─ ID: d9cff78213c7  Tags: [app2:latest] VirtualSize: 478B
    ```
    - _Since all layers of `app1` are now part of `app2`, this is valid output._
    - On deletion of `app2`, all layers of `app1` & `app2` will be deleted from local repository.
- So output referred in https://github.com/containers/libpod/pull/1642#discussion_r225771423 is not possible e.g `unnamed layers`

The above makes sense, as far as a _single_ image and all images with a _single_ tag go.

For completeness, how does the output look if there are two or more images with the same top layer (but possibly different configs)? Just more space-separated values in `Tags:`?

More importantly, is this supposed to support listing more than one image? AFAICS the original `docker images --tree` did, and without that, the “tree” is just a list.  (And if we do support listing two different leaf images at once, the code will again need to deal with unnamed shared parents.) I suppose it could be useful to finish this PR with a single tree branch first, and generalize it separately – as long as this does not confuse users.

for multiple branch images.

```
alp-layer-0  --- alpine:latest  (root)

#### scenario :

   - what should we show in-between image?

      - or maybe a `unnamed`/`<noname:noname>` layer can be added? will it make sense?

## If there is really a “fork”:
… i.e. if `podman images tree` is printing an _actual tree_ with _both_ `app2` and `app3`:

I think that intermediate ”fork” layer, if there is no corresponding tag, should either just not show a name (`Tags: []`), or use some other kind of syntax which is _clearly different from an actual name_.  E.g. a hypothetical `unnamed/noname` would be ambiguous with an actual image using that name (OTOH the proposal which includes the `<`/`>` brackets would be unambiguous because `<` `>` are not valid in tags). Or maybe drop the `Tags: […]` field entirely, making the output less regular but also less cluttered.

It does not make sense to me to show a tag of some derived image (as in your example, the `app2:latest` tag) when that particular layer is _not_ what the tag points to.

For untagged layers, I don’t have a strong opinion on the particular syntax, as long as it is not ambiguous.  `Tags: []` seems simplest to implement at a first glance.

## If we are showing a single image:
It would make sense to me to hide the `app1` “fork” layer and fold it into `app3`, exactly as if `app2` were not present.  (It seems surprising that `podman pull app2` can change the output of `podman tree app3` when `app2` is not a parent of `app3`.)

OTOH a case could be made that there is an utility to showing that a “fork” exists, even if the other branch of the fork is hidden — but in that case, if there is no corresponding tag for _that_ layer, it should be shown without a name, same as in the ‘really a ”fork”’ case above.

Why not use a PR? Reviewing a branch separately, and especially commenting on it, is a bit cumbersome.

---

Looking at https://github.com/kunalkushwaha/libpod/blob/1f7c512849278a29cb7f61158dba3d5dd7afc525/libpod/image/image.go#L1082-L1131 : (Is that the right code?)

- This is yet another, different, output format (unlike the discussion above, it does not collapse unnamed/single-use layers at all)
- Still needs separation between building the tree and formatting it on output, to support different output formats / kinds.
- This changes the set of reported images again, from a single history line to a subtree of all images rooted at the top-level base image of the argument. 
- Just like 
https://github.com/containers/libpod/pull/1642#discussion_r225771423 , both `GetParent` and `GetChildren` may AFAICS return `nil` if there are layers which don’t have any corresponding image. So I can’t see how this can work for many-layer images, at all.
  ```console
   $ sudo bin/podman pull wordpress
   # many layers
   $ sudo bin/podman pull php:7.2-apache
   # a subset of the above layers, clearly a parent (the top shared layer is sha256:90347e981c2dd2e4d4fdacfc8c3067ba027a3d7edf116342d6d06b2ba1789115)
   $ sudo bin/podman image tree wordpress
   └─  ID: e362a3764092  Tags: [docker.io/library/wordpress:latest] VirtualSize: 418.2MB
   # Where are all the parent layers?! At the very least php:7.2-apache should be here
  ```
- Yes, I do like how it reports, or omits, image names, exactly at the top layers of the image, if that’s what you wanted to show by this — but because this code fundamentally can’t handle unnamed layers, this also does not show what you are proposing to do for unnamed layers.

We’ve been talking about unnamed layers for more than a month now, including as recently as 5 hours ago. Didn’t you try a single example with an unnamed layer?!

---

I would prefer to decide what the behavior should be, preferably just in text, and perhaps (but not exclusively) by examples; and _then_ we can talk about implementations.  Sure, “this would be too difficult to implement” is a perfectly valid reason to decide on a design — but “here’s a completely different test program, figure out what it does” is not a productive way to arrive at a decision.

For example, I can’t tell whether the change from printing a single branch as you have proposed in 
https://github.com/containers/libpod/pull/1642#issuecomment-442321524 to printing the whole subtree was intentional, or an unintended implementation artifact.

---

(And while the old versions of the commits proposed in this PR are no longer available, looking at the old review comments, the code is strikingly similar to the very first implementations in this PR. How does it make sense to just ignore the previous discussions and start over again?!)

alp-layer-1  --- alpine:latest

- Since, we are generating these dependencies by parsing layers and not _History of Image_.
- There is no rule, which can tell, which tag is priority among different tags.
e.g. after parsing the layers we get following in structure

   new-layer-1  --- app2:latest
   ```

Why not History:
 - as @mtrmac already explained this earlier in https://github.com/containers/libpod/issues/415#issuecomment-429135936
 
> AFAICS there is a fundamental problem in building the history tags based only on the layer history (ChainID) (which is the only exact way to do that, because the history text is ~arbitrary in principle, and the configs of base images are lost) in that there may exist multiple different images with the same layer set (ChainID) but different configs, and building the history based on ChainID only can’t differentiate between them. (This may not be a problem when the local system has precise “parent image” links, like Docker has for locally-derived (not pulled) images.)

The config is lost, and finding layer-id is unset in most of cases or cannot be assumed if layer data will be available.

@kunalkushwaha, sorry for the silence. The holidays are over and the projects are back to live. If needed, we can set up a call but I fear there are too many open questions to go into details in a call.

Having read the comments above I hope to be able to summarize the remaining pieces that we need to get done until this PR is ready for merging:

- We need to support not only image names but also image IDs.
- The code needs refactoring into a) tree generation (IMHO with a libpod API), b) tree printing (to allow configurable output and different formats).
- Also, we should treat tags correctly. If I do a `podman tag alpine:latest foo:bar` and then a `podman image tree foo:bar` I get `└─ ID: 7bff100f35cb  Tags: [localhost/foo:bar] VirtualSize: 0B`. I expect to see the actual data from `alpine:latest`. In other words, the tree-generation code should always use an ID.
- Layers need to be printed. As far as I can see (also tested), only the top layer/image ID is printed.

There are some discussions in this PR about finding users of a layer etc. but IMHO that does not fall into the scope of an *image tree* for me but rather into an *image lookup* or something.

My personal expectation of a **podman image tree $IMAGE** is that it prints the layer-tree of the specified image. Top down with the following information (which could be made configurable):
**ID, Tags, Created (i.e., time), SIZE**
